summary: Nadia introduces 'Hope in Source,' a ten-episode podcast series she created with open source developer Henry Zhu exploring parallels between faith and open source communities. She observed that religious faith, particularly Christianity, was surprisingly prevalent among open source developers, contrasting with the secular culture of San Francisco tech. The podcast examines how both faith communities and open source projects share organizational patterns around financing, mythology, membership, and public service. Through recording the conversations, Nadia discovered she was less 'non-religious' than she assumed, recognizing her own spiritual practice expressed through her work amplifying creative potential. Both hosts felt vulnerable discussing religion publicly, noting that faith is a topic people in tech deliberately avoid for fear of being labeled. The project was conceived as a finite artifact for shared learning rather than an ongoing media venture.
key_points: Faith is surprisingly prevalent among open source developers, contrasting with the secular norms of mainstream tech culture in places like San Francisco
Open source and organized religion share deep structural parallels: distributed community governance, financing models, mythology and symbolism, and building lasting congregations
Discussing religion openly in tech feels risky because it appends an identity label that people fear will change how others perceive them
The podcast was designed as a finite learning artifact rather than an ongoing media product, challenging the assumption that podcasts must be perpetual
Through exploring faith intellectually, Nadia realized she had her own spiritual practice expressed through her drive to amplify creative potential in the world
money_quotes: Faith and open source both place high value on community and a sense of public service. Organized religion is the original distributed community, and it's lasted thousands of years.

They're organizational systems that bind people tightly together, and whose norms, fascinatingly, didn't derive from any one place, but rather from a shared consensus iterated upon by each passing generation.
Discussing religion can feel like appending a label to your identity, one that will make people see you in an irrevocably different way.
People here who work in tech are fiercely optimistic, and maybe even dogmatic about it, but religion is conspicuously absent from our day-to-day.
I have my own version of spiritual practice; I'm just not used to acknowledging it to others.
We wanted to challenge the idea that podcasts have to be a 'forever' thing.
People worry - rightly so - that openly discussing faith could send the wrong message or invite unwanted attention.
key_insight: Organized religion and open source communities share deep structural parallels as distributed, consensus-driven systems built on public service, yet discussing faith remains taboo in tech culture.
